answer: Node.js handles concurrency using an asynchronous event-driven model instead of OS threads. This means there are no locks, so developers do not need to worry about dead-locking the process. Almost no function in Node.js directly performs I/O, so the process never blocks except when synchronous methods are used. This makes scalable systems reasonable to develop in Node.js.

child_process.fork() and the cluster module; child_process.fork() spawns child processes for communication, while the cluster module allows sharing sockets between processes to enable load balancing over cores.

Node.js exits the event loop when there are no more callbacks to perform. This behavior is different from systems like Ruby's Event Machine or Python's Twisted, where there is always a blocking call to start the event-loop. In Node.js, the event loop is entered automatically after executing the input script and is hidden from the user.
